name: Mojito
curiosity: Mojito comes from mojar, Spanish for “to wet”, and mojo, a Cuban culinary preparation made from lime.
steps: Pour over the rum, stirring with a long-handled spoon. Top up with soda water, garnish with mint and serve.
Muddle the lime juice, sugar and mint leaves in a small jug, crushing the mint as you go – you can use the end of a rolling pin for this. Pour into a tall glass and add a handful of ice.

name: Amaretto Sour
curiosity: Legend has it that Leonardo da Vinci's student Bernardino Luini was gifted a drink made from apricot kernels soaked in brandy by a young, widowed innkeeper.
steps: Mix all the ingredients except the ice and mix until it is pale and starting to increase in volume.
If your blender is suitable for use with ice, add a few handfuls and pulse just twice more to chill the mixture. Alternatively, just stir in the ice until the outside of the jug feels cold.

name: Sidecar
curiosity: The invention of the drink was inspired on an American army captain in Paris during World War I and named after the motorcycle sidecar that the captain used.
steps: Put a coupe glass in the fridge to chill. Tip all of the ingredients into a cocktail shaker.
Shake well, until the outside of the shaker feels cold, then strain the cocktail into the chilled glass. If the lemon juice is too sharp, add the bitters to taste.

name: Aperol Spritz
curiosity: At the beginning of 1900 the Habsburg Empire used to soften Northern Italy wines, with some sparkling water. The verb used to describe it was “spritzen”.
steps: Put a couple of cubes of ice into 2 glasses and add a 50 ml measure of Aperol to each. Divide the prosecco between the glasses and then top up with soda, if you like.

name: Bellini
curiosity: The Bellini was invented sometime between 1934 and 1948 by Giuseppe Cipriani, founder of Harry's Bar in Venice, Italy. He named the drink the Bellini because its unique pink color reminded him of the toga of a saint in a painting by 15th-century Venetian artist Giovanni Bellini.
steps: Put the peach puree in a Champagne flute up to about 1/3 full and slowly top up with Prosecco.

name: Old Fashioned
curiosity: The name comes from the way it is made, with people heading to cocktail bars and requesting for their drink to be created in the 'old fashioned way'.
steps: Put the sugar, bitters and water in a small tumbler. Mix until the sugar dissolves if using granulated.
Fill your glass with ice and stir in the whisky. Add a splash of soda water if you like and mix. Garnish with the orange and cherry.

name: Manhattan
curiosity: According to some sources, it was created at the Manhattan Club, New York. During this era, people ordered a cocktail by the name of the club in which they first had the drink.
steps: Pour all of the ingredients into a mixing glass, add cubed ice and stir.
When the desired dilution has been achieved, strain into a martini coupe.

name: Whiskey Sour
curiosity: For years whiskey sours were enjoyed by sailors on the sea. Once it finally made it to the shores of the United States, its popularity continued. Bartenders today often add egg white to create the classic foam texture.

ingredients.name: Slice of orange and a cherry on a stick

steps: Shake all of the ingredients (except for the lemon zest) hard with ice and strain into an ice-filled rocks glass. Squeeze the lemon zest, shiny side down over the drink so the scented oils spray across the surface. Discard the zest, add the garnish and serve.

name: Godfather
curiosity: The Godfather cocktail allegedly got its name because it was a favourite of Marlon Brando, who was famous for his role as Don Corleone in The Godfather movies.
steps: Pour the whisky and amaretto into a mixing glass or jug. Add a large handful of ice, then stir with a long-handled spoon until the outside of the glass feels very cold. Taste to check the dilution, then fill a tumbler or rocks glass with ice.

Strain the cocktail into the ice-filled glass. Either drop in the strip of orange peel or, to make a twist, trim the ragged edges from the peel and twist into a spiral around a skewer. Hold for a moment, then release gently and drop into the glass.

name: Gin & Tonic
curiosity: Officers in India in the early 19th century mixed gin with quinine tonic to make it more palatable, and the gin and tonic was born.
steps: Mix all ingredients in a glass filled with cubed ice.

name: Cuba Libre
curiosity: In 1900, a captain of the U.S. Army stationed in Havana during the Spanish-American War poured Coca-Cola and a squeeze of lime into his Bacardí and toasted his Cuban comrades by calling out in the bar, “Por Cuba Libre!”
steps: Cut ½ a lime into four small wedges. Squeeze the juice from two of the wedges into a tall glass. Drop the remaining wedges into the glass and fill with ice. Pour in the rum then fill up with cola and stir gently.

name: Martini
curiosity: Maybe it evolved from a cocktail called the Martinez served sometime in the early 1860s at the Occidental Hotel in San Francisco, which people frequented before taking the ferry to the nearby town of Martinez.
steps: Stir the vodka, dry vermouth and a little ice together or put them in a cocktail shaker to combine.
Strain into a chilled martini glass. Serve with an olive on a cocktail stick or a twist of lemon peel.

name: Piña Colada
curiosity: According to the legend, it was invented by a Robert Cofresí who was born rich in Puerto Rico but turned into a pirate when the state began failing in the 1800s.
steps: Pour the pineapple juice into an ice cube tray and freeze until solid.
Pour the white rum into a blender. Add the coconut cream and the pineapple ice cubes and blend together. Pour into tall glasses to serve and garnish with pineapple chunks.

name: Daiquiri
curiosity: It was one of the favourite drinks of writer Ernest Hemingway and President John F. Kennedy. During WW II period whiskey was rationed in US, so Daiquiri helped rum based drinks take off.
steps: Shake all the ingredients in a cocktail shaker and strain into a cocktail glass.

name: Mai Tai
curiosity: In 1944, Trader Vic, was the owner of a tiki restaurant of the same name. After the first sip, his friend exclaimed, "mai tai-roa aé", which in Tahitian means 'out of this world - the best!'

ingredients.name: wedge of pineapple, a lime wheel or a cherry

steps: Pour the golden rum, curacao, orgeat, lime juice and sugar syrup into a cocktail shaker with a handful of ice cubes. Shake until the outside of the shaker feels really cold.
Fill a tumbler with crushed ice then strain the cocktail over it. Slowly pour the dark rum over the top so it stains the top of the drink a rusty gold.

name: Espresso Martini
curiosity: It’s original name was simply vodka espresso, but it became known as a Pharmaceutical Stimulant in the late 1990s.
steps: Mix the sugar syrup in the shaker along with ice, vodka, espresso and the coffee liqueur. Shake until the outside of the cocktail shaker feels icy cold.
Strain into martini chilled glasses. Garnish each one with coffee beans if you like.

name: Cosmopolitan
curiosity: The Cosmopolitan was popularised by the movie Sex and the City, since it was a favourite drink of the actors in the series.
steps: Stir the ingredients and a little ice together or put them in a cocktail shaker to combine. Strain into two chilled martini glasses.

name: Bloody Mary
curiosity: In 1553, Mary Tudor was crowned as the first Queen of England. Her famous religious persecutions lead to her winning the nickname of “Bloody Mary”.

ingredients.name: Celery sticks, lemon wedges and sprinkle of black pepper

steps: Put the ice in a tall glass. Stir all the ingredients together.

name: Black Russian
curiosity: The Cold War was just starting, so creating a dark, mysterious drink by mixing Russian vodka with Coffee Liqueur was appropriate.
steps: Put the vodka and the coffee liqueur into a tumbler filled with ice and stir gently for 30 secs - 1min. Add the cherry and serve. Top with a splash of chilled cola if you prefer a longer drink.

name: Moscow Mule
curiosity: Invented in the '70s as part of a contest at the Oak Beach Inn on Long Island. There is even a mini documentary about its origins.
steps: Add the vodka and load up ¾ full with crushed ice, then fill to the top with the ginger beer and stir gently to combine.
Add a few dashes of ginger bitters. Serve with a sprig of mint to garnish and the lime to squeeze over.

name: Mudslide
curiosity: The original Mudslide was allegedly invented during the 1950s at the Wreck Bar on Grand Cayman in the Cayman Islands. In its heyday the frozen drink was often made with a mix that came in a plastic bottle.
steps: Melt 30g of the chocolate and dip the rim of chilled glasses in them. Stand the glass upright and return to the fridge until you're ready to serve.

Fill a cocktail shaker with all the ingredients (except the chocolate) and shake until the outside of the shaker is very cold. Put a few ice cubes in the prepared glasses, then strain in the cocktail. Finely grate over the remaining chocolate and serve with a paper straw.

name: Appletini
curiosity: This drink, originally called an Adam's Apple Martini was created in 1996 at Lola's West Hollywood restaurant. The drink Adam's Apple was advertised by Smirnoff in the July 1972 issue of Playboy on the inside front cover.
steps: Shake the vodka, cloudy apple juice, lemon juice and maple syrup in an ice-filled shaker until the outside of the shaker feels very cold.
Strain into a martini glass and garnish with a slice of apple.

name: Limoncello
curiosity: The most plausible story states that Limoncello was created at the beginning of the 20th century by Maria Antonia Farace. The woman was the owner of a small hotel in Capri and had a garden full of lemons and oranges that she used to make Limoncello to offer her guests.
steps: Pare the zest from all the lemons, taking care not to include any white pith. Put the zest in a large clean jar and pour over the vodka. Cover with a tightly fitting lid and leave for a week, shaking the jar each day.
Put the sugar in a heatproof bowl and pour over the boiling water, stirring until the sugar has dissolved. Add the vodka and peels and leave for a further week, shaking the jar regularly.
Strain into decorative bottles, adding a few strips of lemon zest to each bottle.

name: Mulled Wine
curiosity: The Romans couldn’t stand the average quality wines, so they invented mulled wine as an efficient way to make unremarkable wine tastier and more aromatic.

ingredients.name: Twist of orange zest and a star anise

steps: Put the muscovado sugar in a pan with 1 star anise, 1 cinnamon stick, 4 cloves and 150ml water. Bring slowly to the boil to dissolve the sugar. and rest.
Add the lemon and clementines, thinly sliced, to the jug along with the Cointreau and the wine. Stir well, then cover and chill for at least 2 hrs or overnight if you can.

name: Sangria
curiosity: Only Spain and Portugal can officially produce and sell Sangria. Similar version might be sold, but they can not have the name “sangria” on them.
steps: Stir the chopped fruit in a bowl and sprinkle over the sugar and cinnamon. Cover and leave in the fridge for at least 1 hr, or ideally overnight.
Fill a large jug with ice. Mix the wine and brandy into the jug with the bowl. Stir, then top up with the sparkling water and serve.

name: Margarita
curiosity: The National Margarita Day is celebrated every year on February 22nd by thousands of Americans and Mexican restaurants around the country.
steps: Put all the ingredients except the lime wedge in a blender and blitz until smooth. Tip into a cocktail, margarita or rocks glass, garnish and serve.

name: Tequila Sunrise
curiosity: No one knows for sure who invented the Tequila Sunrise, but it was listed in bar guides of the 1960s. The Tequila Sunrise was also featured the 1976 novel by Cyra McFaden 'The Serial.'
steps: Pour the grenadine into the base of a tall glass and set aside. Fill a cocktail shaker with ice and add the tequila, triple sec and fruit juices. Shake until the outside of the shaker feels cold.
Add a few ice cubes to glass then carefully double strain the cocktail into it, trying not to disturb the grenadine layer too much.

name: Sex on the Beach
curiosity: A Florida bartender named it after “sex” and “the beach” which he considered to be the two main attractions of Spring Break.

ingredients.name: Glacé cherries and 2 slices of orange

steps: Fill two tall glasses with ice cubes. Pour the vodka, peach schnapps and fruit juices into a large jug and stir.
Divide the mixture between the two glasses and stir gently to combine. Garnish with the cocktail cherries and orange slices.

name: White Russian
curiosity: “The Big Lebowski,” catapulted the drink to cult-classic status. The Dude, drinks eight of them throughout the course of the film.
steps: Put the vodka and the coffee liqueur into a tumbler filled with ice and stir gently for 30 secs - 1min. Add the cherry and serve. Top with a splash of chilled cola if you prefer a longer drink.

name: Long Island
curiosity: Invented in the '70s as part of a contest at the Oak Beach Inn on Long Island. There is even a mini documentary about its origins.
steps: Mix the alcohol into a large (1.5l) jug, and add lime juice. Half fill the jug with ice, then stir until the outside feels cold.
Add the cola then stir to combine. Drop in the lime wedges.

name: Paloma
curiosity: Some believe that it is named after La Paloma (“The Dove”), the popular folk song composed in the early 1860s.

ingredients.name: Sea salt and wedge pink grapefruit

steps: Dip the rim of the glass in water, shake off the excess, then sprinkle a spoonful of salt onto a plate and dip the glass into that to coat the rim.

Fill a cocktail shaker with ice, then add all the remaining ingredients minus the soda water. Shake until the outside of the shaker feels cold, then strain into your glass. Add a few fresh ice cubes and top with soda water to serve.

name: Caipiroska
curiosity: Caipirinha is a cocktail that was born in Brazil in 1918, during the Spanish flu epidemic. Caipirinha refers to someone from the countryside, where the drink was born (Piracicaba).
variations: Replace the Cachaça with Sake (Caipisake). Replace the Cachaça with Vodka (Caipiroska).
steps: Cut the lime into small chunks, then put it into the bottom of a sturdy tumbler and add the golden granulated sugar. Crush really well with a pestle.
Top up the tumbler with crushed ice, then add the vodka. Stir well to mix all the ingredients together, and serve

name: Aviation
curiosity: Named thanks to the crème de violette giving the cocktail a pale sky-blue colour. It dates from the early age of aeronautics when air travel was a glamorous luxury that only the rich could afford.
steps: Mix all the ingredients into a cocktail shaker and shake well. Taste, then add a splash of chilled water to dilute, if you like. Strain into four chilled cocktail glasses and garnish with blueberries and lemon zest.

name: Mimosa
curiosity: The origin story of the Mimosa is somewhat debated, most credit Frank Meier, a bartender at the Ritz Hotel in Paris, with creating the cocktail in 1925. The cocktail is named after the yellow-flowered mimosa plant, Acacia dealbata.
steps: Half-fill each of 6 glasses with champagne, then carefully pour in the orange juice until the glass is full.

name: Tom Collins
curiosity: Named after a silly joke, a bartender created the drink, so if anyone came rushing into his bar seeking revenge and asking for Tom Collins, they would have ordered a tall gin drink instead.
variations: Gently muddling a handful of raspberries in the bottom and garnish with an orange slice. Use a young-ish VS or VSOP cognac that’s nice and fruity instead of gin, then garnish with a slice of ginger or a ribbon of lemon peel Mix the drink as normal, but use the liqueur or cordial in place of the sugar syrup. Garnish with a lemon slice.
steps: Build the drink over plenty of ice in a Collins glass, stir gently and garnish with a slice of lemon.

name: Negroni
curiosity: Is believed to have been invented in Florence, Italy, in a café previously called Café Casoni. Now, the supposed birthplace of the famed cocktail goes by Giacosa Café. Although the cafe has closed, you can still visit a plaque in the former birthplace of the Negroni.
steps: Pour the gin, vermouth and Campari into a mixing glass or jug with ice. Stir well until the outside of the glass feels cold.
Strain into a tumbler and add 1 large ice sphere or some fresh ice, and garnish with an orange slice, using a blood orange when in season.
